preamble: The shift from 'dh' (ð) to 'z' is the voiced counterpart of sibilantization, turning a voiced dental fricative into a voiced sibilant alveolar fricative.

examples.note: Sibilantization of the classical voiced dental fricative.
Commonly observed in European speakers of English as a second language.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
